title: ML Platform Design — Feature Stores, Skew & Real-Time Joins
description: Enterprise-scale ML systems demand robust platforms. Staff Engineers must design systems that eliminate training-serving skew, manage features in real-time, execute point-in-time correct joins, and optimize feature serving below 5ms at 100K+ QPS.

sections.title: ML Platform Architecture: Michelangelo to FBLearner
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A modern ML platform decouples the ML workflow into clean modules: **Feature Management (Feature Store)**, **Model Registry**, **Distributed Training Execution**, and **Model Serving Engine**. Systems like Meta's FBLearner and Uber's Michelangelo emphasize standardized pipelines so that engineers write configuration rather than custom infrastructure boilerplate.

bar_chart Throughput Target: Production feature stores at FAANG scale must serve online features with p99 latency < 5ms at 1M+ QPS. This requires in-memory caching layers (e.g. Redis Cluster, Aerospike) sitting in front of offline data lakes (e.g. BigQuery, Snowflake, Apache Iceberg).

search Interview Angle: 'Design an ML platform for a rideshare app that matches drivers and riders in real-time.' Focus on the separation between batch features (e.g., driver's historical acceptance rate over 30 days) and streaming features (e.g., driver's location update 5 seconds ago). They must merge seamlessly into a single feature vector at inference time.

sections.title: Point-in-Time Correct Joins (Temporal Joins)
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: When generating training data, we must join features to labels based on the exact timestamp the event occurred. If a user was shown an ad at 12:00, we must join the features exactly as they existed at 12:00. Joining with feature values from 12:05 introduces **data leakage** (future information leaking into the past), leading to inflated model metrics in training but poor real-world performance.

bar_chart Leakage Impact: Leakage in click-prediction models can falsely boost offline ROC-AUC metrics from 0.70 to 0.95, while online conversion drops to zero because the model depended on future-leaked action events.

track_changes Code pattern: Use AS-OF joins (temporal window joins) where each label matches the latest feature timestamp that is less than or equal to the label timestamp.
sections.codeBlock:
import pandas as pd

def point_in_time_join(labels_df, features_df, entity_key, time_key):
    """
    Performs a point-in-time correct join to prevent data leakage.
    """
    # Sort dataframes by time for merge_asof
    labels_df = labels_df.sort_values(by=time_key)
    features_df = features_df.sort_values(by=time_key)
    
    # merge_asof matches on nearest key in features_df that is <= labels_df key
    joined_df = pd.merge_asof(
        labels_df,
        features_df,
        on=time_key,
        by=entity_key,
        direction='backward'  # backward ensures match_time <= event_time
    )
    return joined_df

# Example usage:
# labels: [user_1, click, 12:00:05]
# features: [user_1, credit_score=700, 11:59:00], [user_1, credit_score=710, 12:01:00]
# Point-in-time join yields credit_score=700 (correct historical state)

sections.title: Training-Serving Skew Detection & Mitigation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Training-Serving Skew is a difference between performance during training and performance during inference. It is caused by: 1) **Data pipeline differences**: Features computed using different SQL scripts in batch training vs. Java/C++ in serving. 2) **Time shift**: Training data represents stale distributions. 3) **Feedback loop bias**: Serving models filter the data that is later collected for training.

track_changes Mitigation: Log the exact feature values computed at serving time directly to a logging database (e.g. BigTable/Kinesis) and reuse *those logged values* to create training datasets, rather than recompute features from raw database logs retrospectively.

search Interview Case: 'A model predicts CTR correctly offline, but performance drops 40% when served. How do you debug?' Answer: Set up feature logging at serving. Calculate the statistical difference (e.g., Jensen-Shannon divergence or mean differences) between serving-logged feature distributions and training feature distributions.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from scipy.spatial.distance import jensenshannon

def calculate_feature_skew(training_feature, serving_feature, num_bins=20):
    """
    Quantify skew between training and serving distributions using Jensen-Shannon Divergence.
    """
    # Create unified bin edges
    min_val = min(training_feature.min(), serving_feature.min())
    max_val = max(training_feature.max(), serving_feature.max())
    bins = np.linspace(min_val, max_val, num_bins)
    
    # Compute probability distributions
    train_hist, _ = np.histogram(training_feature, bins=bins, density=True)
    serve_hist, _ = np.histogram(serving_feature, bins=bins, density=True)
    
    # Add small epsilon to avoid divide-by-zero
    p = train_hist + 1e-9
    q = serve_hist + 1e-9
    
    # JSD is symmetric, bounded [0, 1]. > 0.1 indicates significant skew.
    return jensenshannon(p, q)

sections.title: High-Throughput, Low-Latency Serving: Feast Architecture
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A feature store maintains two stores. The **Offline Store** (e.g. Snowflake, Hive) stores historical feature values for training data generation (optimized for high throughput scans). The **Online Store** (e.g. Redis, Cassandra) stores only the *latest* feature value for every entity (optimized for low-latency point lookups during inference).

bar_chart Latency Comparison: Querying 100 features for a batch of 50 users: offline SQL database: ~800ms. Online Redis cluster lookup: ~3ms.

track_changes Operational Pattern: Feature ingestion pipelines run asynchronously (e.g., Spark streaming job or Flink) writing update records to Redis. Inference servers must never update feature store states directly.

sections.title: FAANG Staff+ Q&A: ML Platforms
sections.description:
Q: What is the benefit of a Dual-database Feature Store?
A: It matches data architectures to access workloads. Offline databases (columnar storage like BigQuery or Parquet files) optimize analytical query scans over millions of rows to construct training sets. Online databases (key-value storage like Redis or DynamoDB) optimize sub-millisecond retrievals for real-time inference. De-coupling them ensures online serving is isolated from training resource contention.

Q: How do you handle cold-start entities in an online feature store?
A: Have a fallback hierarchy: 1) Retrieve default/global feature averages. 2) Fall back to cohort/categorical averages (e.g., if user_id is new, use average values of their geo-location or sign-up device cohort). 3) Compute lightweight real-time defaults on the serving client. Log these fallbacks to track feature coverage metrics.

details: Staff Engineer Summary: Scalable ML requires feature stores. Implementing dual-store architectures (Feast/Tecton pattern) enables low-latency retrieval. Always log serving-time feature values to prevent training-serving skew, and use temporal joins (merge_asof) to avoid data leakage in offline training datasets.
